The rate has held in a narrow 10.7%–11.1% band across all four periods, showing stable but unspectacular momentum.
While growth is positive, it lags vehicle loans (16.5%) and is dwarfed by gold loans (127.6%), explaining the share erosion.
sibc-pl-housing-yoy.inference: Housing loan growth is steady but unremarkable. The 11% pace is healthy in absolute terms but insufficient to defend market share against faster-growing categories. Lenders relying on housing for retail growth will need to accept slower portfolio expansion or pivot toward higher-velocity segments.

sibc-pl-other-share.title: Other Personal Loans share drops to 24.8% — new low
sibc-pl-other-share.observation: Other Personal Loans represented 24.8% of personal loans in December 2025, the lowest share in this four-period window.
sibc-pl-other-share.direction: Share fell from 25.7% in March 2025 to 24.8% in December 2025, down 0.9 points.
chain: Other Personal Loans share declined to 24.8% in December 2025, the minimum in this series.
The 0.9-point drop from March marks the steepest single-period decline, continuing a steady erosion from 25.8% in January.
Despite positive YoY growth, the category is losing share to faster-expanding segments like gold and vehicle loans.
sibc-pl-other-share.inference: Other Personal Loans are growing but not fast enough to hold share in a rapidly shifting retail mix. The category's structural decline suggests borrower demand or lender appetite is tilting toward secured products (gold, vehicle) and away from unsecured or miscellaneous lending. Lenders with heavy exposure here face margin pressure as mix shifts.

sibc-pl-other-yoy.title: Other Personal Loans jump to 10.1% YoY — highest yet
sibc-pl-other-yoy.observation: Other Personal Loans grew 10.1% year-on-year in December 2025, the strongest rate in this four-period window.
sibc-pl-other-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 7.5% in March 2025 to 10.1% in December 2025, adding 2.6 points.
chain: Other Personal Loans grew 10.1% YoY in December 2025, up from 7.5% in March — the largest single-period jump in this series.
The 2.6-point acceleration marks a sharp reversal from the prior deceleration trend (8.2% → 7.5%).
However, 10.1% growth still trails vehicle loans (16.5%) and is a fraction of gold loans (127.6%), explaining the share loss despite positive growth.
sibc-pl-other-yoy.inference: Other Personal Loans have re-accelerated but remain a laggard in the retail growth race. The category is growing in absolute terms but losing competitive position. Lenders should monitor whether this acceleration sustains or fades — if it holds, it may stabilize share; if not, further erosion is likely.

sibc-pl-share-advances-share.title: Advances against shares at 0.2% — negligible share
sibc-pl-share-advances-share.observation: Advances against shares and bonds accounted for 0.2% of personal loans in December 2025, unchanged from prior periods.
sibc-pl-share-advances-share.direction: Share held steady at 0.2% across all four periods, with a marginal 0.02-point decline from March to December.
chain: Advances against shares remained at 0.2% of personal loans in December 2025, the smallest category in the retail book.
The share has been flat across the entire window, indicating minimal activity and no structural change.
This category is immaterial to overall retail credit dynamics.
sibc-pl-share-advances-share.inference: Advances against shares are a rounding error in the retail portfolio. Lenders can safely ignore this segment for strategic planning — it contributes negligible volume and shows no signs of expansion.

sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.title: Advances against shares slow to 4.1% YoY — new low
sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.observation: Advances against shares and bonds grew 4.1% year-on-year in December 2025, the weakest rate in this four-period series.
sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated sharply from 18.7% in March 2025 to 4.1% in December 2025, down 14.6 points.
chain: Advances against shares grew just 4.1% YoY in December 2025, down from 18.7% in March — a 14.6-point collapse.
This is the steepest single-period deceleration in the dataset, following a prior drop from 33.0% in January to 18.7% in March.
The category has gone from the fastest-growing in January to the slowest in December, though its 0.2% share means the impact is negligible.
sibc-pl-share-advances-yoy.inference: Advances against shares have lost all momentum, but the category is too small to matter. The sharp deceleration may reflect regulatory scrutiny or lender caution on securities-backed lending, but with 0.2% share, it has no material impact on retail credit strategy.

sibc-pl-share-scan.title: Housing at 49.0%, gold at 5.8% — share shifts underway
sibc-pl-share-scan.observation: Housing loans held 49.0% of personal loans in December 2025, while gold loans reached 5.8%, with a 48.8-point spread between the largest and smallest categories.
sibc-pl-share-scan.direction: Housing and Other Personal Loans both shifted to weakening status, losing 1.4 and 0.9 points respectively, while gold loans strengthened, gaining share.
chain: Housing (49.0%) and Other Personal Loans (24.8%) are both weakening, together accounting for 73.8% of the retail book but losing ground.
Gold loans (5.8%) shifted to strengthening status, gaining share despite being the fifth-largest category.
Vehicle loans (10.7%) are active and stable, while the remaining five categories (credit cards, fixed deposits, education, durables, shares) are small and largely static.
sibc-pl-share-scan.inference: The retail book is undergoing a structural rebalancing: the two largest categories (housing, other) are losing share to faster-growing secured products (gold, vehicle). Lenders with heavy housing/unsecured exposure face mix headwinds. Those with gold and vehicle franchises are gaining competitive advantage. The 48.8-point spread shows extreme concentration — the top two categories still dominate, but their grip is loosening.

sibc-pl-vehicle-share.title: Vehicle loans reach 10.7% share — highest on record
sibc-pl-vehicle-share.observation: Vehicle loans accounted for 10.7% of personal loans in December 2025, the largest share in this four-period window.
sibc-pl-vehicle-share.direction: Share rose from 10.4% in March 2025 to 10.7% in December 2025, up 0.3 points.
chain: Vehicle loans reached 10.7% share in December 2025, the highest level in this series.
The 0.3-point gain from March is modest but marks the third consecutive period at or near the maximum.
The share expansion is driven by 16.5% YoY growth, well above the housing (11.1%) and other personal loans (10.1%) pace.
sibc-pl-vehicle-share.inference: Vehicle loans are gaining share steadily, supported by strong double-digit growth. The category is becoming a more important part of the retail mix, offering lenders a secured, mid-ticket alternative to housing. Continued share gains are likely if the 16.5% growth pace holds.

sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.title: Vehicle loans surge to 16.5% YoY — highest yet
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.observation: Vehicle loans grew 16.5% year-on-year in December 2025, the strongest rate in this four-period window.
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated sharply from 8.6% in March 2025 to 16.5% in December 2025, adding 7.9 points.
chain: Vehicle loans grew 16.5% YoY in December 2025, up from 8.6% in March — a 7.9-point jump, the largest acceleration in this series.
The rate nearly doubled in a single period, shifting from near the bottom of the range to the maximum.
This is the second-fastest growing category after gold loans (127.6%), and the acceleration is broad-based, not a one-off spike.
sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy.inference: Vehicle loans have hit an inflection point, with growth nearly doubling in one period. This is the strongest signal of momentum shift in the retail book outside of gold. Lenders with vehicle finance capabilities should lean in — the category is gaining share, growing fast, and offers secured exposure with lower ticket sizes than housing. The 7.9-point acceleration suggests demand is surging, likely driven by post-pandemic mobility needs and improving consumer sentiment.

sibc-pl-yoy-scan.title: Gold at 127.6%, vehicle at 16.5% — growth polarizing
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.observation: Gold loans grew 127.6% YoY in December 2025, while vehicle loans hit 16.5%, with a 132.8-point spread between the fastest and slowest categories.
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.direction: Vehicle loans, housing, and other personal loans all shifted to strengthening status, while gold loans moved to active after a prior strengthening phase.
chain: Gold loans (127.6%) remain in a league of their own, growing 8× faster than the second-place vehicle loans (16.5%).
Vehicle loans, housing (11.1%), and other personal loans (10.1%) all strengthened, showing broad-based acceleration across mid-tier categories.
Credit cards (1.0%) and consumer durables (-5.1%) are the only laggards, with durables in outright decline.
sibc-pl-yoy-scan.inference: Retail credit growth is bifurcating: gold loans are in hypergrowth, vehicle/housing/other are accelerating in the low-to-mid teens, and credit cards/durables are stagnant or contracting. Lenders should prioritize gold and vehicle exposure for growth, maintain housing for stability, and reassess unsecured/durables strategies. The 132.8-point spread is the widest in this dataset — growth is concentrating in secured, asset-backed products while unsecured categories struggle.
